ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai boat noodle with chicken. FOODON_00002700 (noodle soup food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent; no specific Thai boat noodle with chicken code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Thai boat noodle with chicken as a specific food concept.

main_ingredients: pork blood (traditional; may be omitted in Singapore variant)
Thai herbs (galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken boat noodle as served in a Thai restaurant in Singapore, approximately 400 g including broth, noodles, chicken slices, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Boat noodle, chicken — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai noodle soup with chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai chicken boat noodle exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for chicken noodle soup (FDC ID 172934, 'Soup, chicken noodle, canned, condensed' adjusted for restaurant-style preparation) with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Thai-style noodle soups. The dish is approximately 55% broth, 20% noodles, 15% chicken, 10% vegetables. Per-100g values are derived from this weighted composition. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning typical of Thai boat noodle.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and chicken noodle soup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai chicken boat noodle. GI estimated at 45 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI ~53, low category) and chicken noodle soup (GI ~38–45). The dish is predominantly rice noodles in a protein-rich broth; protein and fat content moderate the glycemic response. A blended GI of 45 is used as a conservative low-GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 30 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 13.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of chicken boat noodle due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1680 mg per 400 g bowl), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or reduced-broth portions is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from rice noodles with low-to-moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken boat noodle has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) due to the protein-rich broth and moderate noodle portion, but the glycemic load per bowl (~13.5) is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, choose smaller noodle portions, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1680 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~2 g per bowl) in chicken boat noodle are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, opt for lean chicken breast, and request reduced-broth servings to lower sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken boat noodle (Thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือไก่, romanized: Kuai Tiao Ruea Kai) is a traditional Thai noodle soup dish originating from the floating markets of central Thailand, where vendors sold noodles from small boats along the canals. The dish features tender slices of chicken — typically breast or thigh — served in a deeply aromatic, dark broth made from chicken stock seasoned with fish sauce, dark soy sauce, galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and dried spices. The broth is characteristically rich, slightly sweet, and savoury with a complex herbal depth. Rice noodles (sen lek or sen yai) form the base, accompanied by bean sprouts, morning glory (water spinach), fried garlic, and dried chilli flakes. In Singapore, the dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Saap Saap Thai and is typically priced around SGD 11.90 per bowl. Nutritionally, a 400 g bowl provides approximately 272 kcal, 23 g protein, 7 g fat, and 30 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1680 mg per bowl) due to fish sauce and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management when portion size is controlled. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared without pork blood.

What is Kuai Tiao Ruea Kai?

How much protein is in chicken boat noodle?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai chicken boat noodle was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central chicken noodle soup data and FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai noodle soup composition data using a weighted component approach. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles and chicken noodle soup. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based estimation methodology is defensible and the dish composition is well-characterised.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (chicken noodle soup, FDC ID 172934) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai noodle soup data using a weighted composition model (55% broth, 20% noodles, 15% chicken, 10% vegetables). Direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish from Saap Saap Thai - Funan is not available. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish category. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish category. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed as negligible/trace in the source data for this dish type. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (45, low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice noodles (~53) and chicken noodle soup (~38–45); no direct GI measurement for Thai chicken boat noodle was found. 7. Halal status is flagged as true for the chicken variant; traditional boat noodle may contain pork blood which would affect halal status — the Singapore restaurant variant (Saap Saap Thai) is assumed to use a halal-compliant recipe. 8. The food_name has been normalised to 'Chicken Boat Noodle' (canonical English form); the input menu name 'N4 Chicken Boat Noodle' is preserved as an alias.
